Angular

JavaScript

Typescript

Web Development

Software Engineering

12 min read
16 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A senior engineer’s honest account of adopting micro frontends too early, discovering webpack’s limits too late, and what the right architecture actually looks like in 2026.

The Decision That Seemed Obvious at the Time
In 2022 our engineering team was growing. What had started as a single Angular application — a financial operations platform — had become a coordination problem. Three teams. One codebase. Every release required a meeting. Every deployment required sign-off from people who had nothing to do with the change being shipped.
The solution seemed obvious: micro frontends.
We had read the architecture blogs. We had watched the conference talks. We understood the promise — independent teams, independent deployments, team autonomy at the UI layer. We chose Webpack Module Federation, because in 2022 it was the only credible option for Angular, and we got to work.
Eighteen months later the system was running. Teams could deploy independently. The release coordination meeting was gone. By every metric we had set, the migration had succeeded.
Then Angular 17 shipped esbuild as the default build tool.
Our colleagues on other teams ran ng serve and reported build times under a minute. Hot reload that had taken 8–12 seconds was now 2–3 seconds. Our build times stayed the same. We were locked to webpack — not because webpack was wrong, but because Module Federation required it, and there was no path to esbuild while staying on Module Federation.
We had solved the team coordination problem. We had accidentally created a build performance problem that would follow us for two more years.
This article is what I would have done differently, and what the right architecture looks like in 2026.

Part 1 — The Honest Cost-Benefit of Micro Frontends
Before showing any code, I want to be direct about something that most micro frontend articles skip entirely.
Micro frontends are expensive. Not in cloud infrastructure or licensing — in engineering complexity, coordination overhead, and cognitive load. The benefits are real. So are the costs. Teams that adopt micro frontends without understanding both end up solving the wrong problem with a very expensive tool.
The Benefits — When They Are Real
Independent deployment. The most valuable benefit. A change in the orders domain can be built, tested, and deployed without touching the dashboard or the reporting modules. Release risk is isolated. Deployment frequency can increase per team without increasing cross-team coordination.
Team autonomy. Each team owns their domain end-to-end — the component library, the routing, the state management, the test strategy. They can make technical decisions within their domain without committee approval.
Domain isolation. A bug in the reporting micro frontend cannot crash the orders micro frontend. Failures are contained.
The Costs — What Nobody Told Us
Distributed ownership is harder than it looks. When one team’s remote breaks the shell, who is responsible? When shared dependencies version-drift, who resolves it? When a cross-domain feature requires changes in two remotes and the shell, who coordinates the release? These questions need clear answers before you deploy a single remote. We did not have clear answers and paid for it in incident response time.
Shared state is genuinely hard. Two remotes that need to know who is logged in cannot import from each other. They need a shared contract — a library that both depend on, federated as a singleton. Getting this right requires deliberate design. Getting it wrong means authentication state that the shell knows but the remote does not, and debugging sessions that span two separate applications.
Deployment coordination is not eliminated — it is transformed. You no longer coordinate on every release. But you do coordinate on shared dependency versions, on breaking API changes between shell and remote, and on the federation manifest that maps remote names to deployed URLs. The coordination is less frequent but more architecturally significant when it does happen.
The onboarding cost is real. A new developer joining a micro frontend system needs to understand not just the application they are working on, but the federation protocol, the shared contract, the manifest-based runtime loading, and the debugging workflow for cross-application issues. This is a non-trivial investment.
The Decision Tree
Use this before making any architecture decision:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Do you need micro frontends? Enterprise decision framework — 2026Part 2 — Why Webpack Module Federation Was Right in 2022 and Wrong in 2026
What Module Federation Got Right
Webpack Module Federation solved a genuinely hard problem. Before it, sharing code between separately-deployed Angular applications required either publishing shared packages to npm (slow, heavy) or duplicating code (unsustainable). Module Federation allowed a remote application to expose modules that a shell could consume at runtime — no package publishing, no code duplication.
The Angular-architects @angular-architects/module-federation library wrapped this in an Angular-friendly API and became the de facto standard.
// What our webpack config looked like — every app had one of these
const ModuleFederationPlugin = require('webpack/lib/container/ModuleFederationPlugin');module.exports = {
plugins: [
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
name: 'orders',
filename: 'remoteEntry.js',
exposes: {
'./OrdersModule': './src/app/orders/orders.module.ts'
},
shared: {
'@angular/core': { singleton: true, strictVersion: true },
'@angular/common': { singleton: true, strictVersion: true },
'@angular/router': { singleton: true, strictVersion: true }
}
})
]
};This worked. For two years, it worked well.
What Changed
Angular 16 introduced esbuild as an experimental option. Angular 17 made it the default. Angular 18 deprecated the webpack-based build pipeline for new projects.
The Angular team’s direction was unambiguous: esbuild is the future. Webpack is the legacy path.
Module Federation requires webpack. The ApplicationBuilder — Angular’s esbuild-based builder — does not support Module Federation. They are incompatible at the architectural level.
Teams on Module Federation in 2024 faced a choice:

• Stay on webpack. Keep Module Federation. Lose esbuild build speeds. Accept that every Angular CLI improvement built around esbuild would not benefit them.

• Migrate to Native Federation. Gain esbuild. Rewrite federation configuration. Invest the migration time.
We chose to stay on webpack through 2024. It was the wrong call. The build performance gap between our team and the rest of the organisation widened every quarter.
Mistake: We treated the webpack lock-in as a known, acceptable limitation rather than an accumulating cost. Every month we stayed on webpack was a month of slower developer experience that compounded into slower feature delivery.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

From Webpack Module Federation to Native Federation — Angular Evolution 2022–2026
Part 3 — Native Federation: The Right Default in 2026
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Native Federation Architecture — Shell host dynamically loads three independent remote applications at runtimeNative Federation — from @angular-architects/native-federation — uses browser-native ECMAScript modules and Import Maps instead of webpack’s runtime. It integrates with the Angular CLI’s ApplicationBuilder and uses esbuild directly.
The result: the same logical architecture — shell loads remotes at runtime — built on standards rather than bundler internals.
Workspace Setup
# Create workspace without a default application
ng new enterprise-portal --no-create-application
cd enterprise-portal# Generate shell and remotes
ng generate application shell --routing
ng generate application orders-mfe --routing
ng generate application dashboard-mfe --routing# Add Native Federation
ng add @angular-architects/native-federation --project shell --port 4200 --type dynamic-host
ng add @angular-architects/native-federation --project orders-mfe --port 4201 --type remote
ng add @angular-architects/native-federation --project dashboard-mfe --port 4202 --type remoteThe dynamic-host type is important. It means the shell resolves remote URLs at runtime from a manifest file — not hardcoded at build time. This is “build once, deploy everywhere.”
Remote Configuration
// projects/orders-mfe/federation.config.js
const { withNativeFederation, shareAll } = require('@angular-architects/native-federation/config');module.exports = withNativeFederation({
name: 'orders',
exposes: {
'./OrdersRoutes': './projects/orders-mfe/src/app/orders.routes.ts'
},
shared: {
...shareAll({ singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' })
},
skip: ['rxjs/ajax', 'rxjs/fetch', 'rxjs/testing', 'rxjs/webSocket', 'zone.js']
});Shell Configuration
// projects/shell/federation.config.js
const { withNativeFederation, shareAll } = require('@angular-architects/native-federation/config');module.exports = withNativeFederation({
name: 'shell',
remotes: {
'orders': 'http://localhost:4201/remoteEntry.json',
'dashboard': 'http://localhost:4202/remoteEntry.json'
},
shared: {
...shareAll({ singleton: true, strictVersion: true, requiredVersion: 'auto' })
},
skip: ['rxjs/ajax', 'rxjs/fetch', 'rxjs/testing', 'rxjs/webSocket', 'zone.js']
});The Runtime Manifest — Build Once, Deploy Everywhere
// projects/shell/src/assets/federation.manifest.json
{
"orders": "http://localhost:4201/remoteEntry.json",
"dashboard": "http://localhost:4202/remoteEntry.json"
}In production, your CI/CD pipeline replaces this file with environment-specific URLs. The shell binary never changes between environments — only the manifest does.
// Production manifest — injected by CI/CD at deploy time
{
"orders": "https://orders.yourdomain.com/remoteEntry.json",
"dashboard": "https://dashboard.yourdomain.com/remoteEntry.json"
}This is a meaningful improvement over Module Federation, where remote URLs were typically baked into environment files at build time — meaning a separate build per environment.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Shell Bootstrap and Routing
// projects/shell/src/main.ts
import { initFederation } from '@angular-architects/native-federation';initFederation('/assets/federation.manifest.json')
.catch(err => console.error('Federation init failed:', err))
.then(() => import('./bootstrap'))
.catch(err => console.error('Bootstrap failed:', err));// projects/shell/src/app/app.routes.ts
import { loadRemoteModule } from '@angular-architects/native-federation';
import { Routes } from '@angular/router';export const routes: Routes = [
{
path: 'orders',
loadChildren: () =>
loadRemoteModule('orders', './OrdersRoutes')
.then(m => m.ORDERS_ROUTES)
},
{
path: 'dashboard',
loadChildren: () =>
loadRemoteModule('dashboard', './DashboardRoutes')
.then(m => m.DASHBOARD_ROUTES)
}
];Recommendation: Always use loadChildren with a routes file rather than loadComponent for remote entry points. Routes files give you fine-grained lazy loading within the remote — the remote itself only loads the specific component the user navigates to, not the entire remote application.

Part 4 — Production Lessons: What the Architecture Guides Miss
Shared Authentication — The Right Pattern
This is the most common source of bugs in Angular micro frontend systems. The shell authenticates the user. The remote needs to know who that user is. They cannot import from each other.
The correct pattern: a shared library published within the monorepo, federated as a singleton.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Wrong vs Correct — singleton: true is the difference between state mismatch and reliable shared auth// libs/shared-auth/src/lib/auth.store.ts
import { Injectable, signal, computed } from '@a
